sections.heading: Nodes
sections.body: In a semantic network, nodes represent concepts or objects — for example, "Dog," "Animal," or a specific individual like "Rex." The network as a whole is a graph structure built from these nodes.

sections.heading: Links
sections.body: Links (edges) connect nodes and represent relationships between them — such as "is-a" (Rex is-a Dog), "has-part," or "has-property" — the meaning of the network comes as much from its links as from its nodes.

sections.heading: Inheritance
sections.body: Semantic networks commonly support inheritance through "is-a" links: if Dog is-a Animal, and Animal has-property "can move," then Dog (and any specific dog) inherits that property too, without it needing to be stated explicitly for every node.

sections.heading: Relationships
sections.body: Beyond simple hierarchy, semantic networks can capture many kinds of relationships between concepts — part-whole relationships, causal relationships, and general associations — giving a richer, more connected picture of a domain than a flat list of isolated facts.

sections.heading: Knowledge Graph
sections.body: A knowledge graph is a large-scale, practical realization of semantic-network ideas — used in modern systems (like search engines) to represent and connect real-world entities and their relationships at massive scale, powering features like structured search results.

quiz.question: In a semantic network, what do nodes typically represent?
What does an "is-a" link typically enable?
options: Inheritance of properties from a general category to a more specific one

quiz.question: A knowledge graph is best described as:
options: A large-scale realization of semantic-network ideas connecting real-world entities and relationships
